column6: Short Note (Simplified)
column12: License

column6: Fort Cornwallis is one of George Town’s key colonial landmarks. It represents the city’s early British presence and its strategic role in trade, defence, and port administration.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page

column6: Khoo Kongsi is a major Chinese clan house in George Town. It reflects Hokkien migration, family networks, community organisation, and southern Chinese architectural craftsmanship.
column12: CC BY-SA 3.0

column6: Kapitan Keling Mosque is a historic mosque associated with South Indian Muslim traders. It shows how religion, migration, and commerce shaped George Town’s multicultural urban landscape.
column7: Kapitan Keling Mosque, Georgetown, Penang, Malaysia.JPG
column12: Commons free license; see file page

column6: Cheong Fatt Tze Mansion is a restored late nineteenth-century residence known for its indigo-blue façade. It combines Chinese courtyard planning with imported decorative and architectural features.
column12: Dual license; see Commons file page

column5: Historic commercial street and living heritage
column6: Armenian Street is a historic street within George Town’s old town. Its shophouses, cultural sites, and public art make it a visible example of living urban heritage.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page

column6: Pinang Peranakan Mansion presents the material culture of the Straits Chinese community. The site helps explain Peranakan identity through architecture, furniture, objects, and domestic life.
column7: Penang Peranakan Mansion by C.W. Tan 11.jpg
column12: CC BY-SA 4.0

column6: St. George’s Church is one of the oldest Anglican churches in Southeast Asia. Its neoclassical form reflects British colonial religious and civic influence in George Town.
column12: CC BY-SA 3.0

column5: Chinese popular religion and community life
column6: The Goddess of Mercy Temple is one of George Town’s oldest Chinese temples. It remains an important place for worship, community gathering, and everyday religious practice.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page

column6: Sri Mahamariamman Temple is a historic Hindu temple in Little India. Its colourful gateway and ritual role reflect the cultural presence of the Tamil community in George Town.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page

column6: Acheen Street Mosque was founded in the late eighteenth century. It reflects Islamic trading networks and the role of George Town as a regional hub for Muslim communities.
column12: CC BY-SA 3.0

column5: Chinese migrant labour and waterfront settlement
column6: The Clan Jetties are waterfront settlements built on stilts over the sea. They connect George Town’s port history with migrant labour, clan identity, and everyday maritime life.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page

column5: Anglo-Indian architecture and colonial domestic life
column6: Suffolk House is a restored Anglo-Indian garden house linked to Penang’s early colonial period. It shows how European domestic architecture was adapted to a tropical setting.
column12: CC BY-SA 3.0

column5: Revolutionary networks and Chinese diaspora politics
column6: Sun Yat Sen Museum preserves a shophouse connected to Sun Yat-sen’s revolutionary movement. It links George Town to wider Southeast Asian political and diaspora networks.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page

column2: Penang State Museum and Art Gallery
column6: Penang State Museum and Art Gallery preserves objects, artworks, and records related to Penang’s social and cultural history. It supports public understanding of the state’s multicultural past.
column12: CC BY-SA 3.0

column6: Dhammikarama Burmese Temple is a historic Burmese Buddhist temple in Penang. It shows the presence of Burmese Buddhist communities and cross-regional religious connections.
column12: CC BY-SA 3.0

column6: Wat Chaiya Mangalaram is a Thai Buddhist temple known for its large reclining Buddha. It reflects Thai Buddhist presence and cross-cultural religious life in Penang.
column12: CC BY 2.0

column6: Penang City Hall is a colonial-era civic building facing the Esplanade. It represents British municipal administration and the development of George Town as an urban centre.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page

column5: Colonial public assembly and social life
column6: Penang Town Hall is a nineteenth-century civic building next to City Hall. It was historically associated with public events, colonial social life, and urban administration.
column12: CC BY-SA 4.0

column6: Han Jiang Ancestral Temple is associated with the Teochew community in Penang. Its architecture and institutional role reflect clan identity, association life, and migrant support networks.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page

column5: Conflict memory and clan neighbourhood space
column6: Cannon Square is a public space within the Khoo Kongsi area. It connects clan architecture with urban memory, neighbourhood life, and the history of conflict in George Town.
column12: Author requests attribution; see file page

column6: Penang Islamic Museum documents the spread and development of Islam in Penang. Its building and collection connect religious history with Malay, Achehnese, and regional networks.
column12: CC BY 2.5 or CC BY-SA 3.0 options on file page

column6: Nagore Square is linked to Indian Muslim heritage and urban community life. It reflects how religious memory, trade, and shophouse streetscapes contribute to George Town’s cultural diversity.
column12: CC BY-SA 3.0

column6: Batu Ferringhi represents Penang’s coastal landscape and everyday maritime life. It helps extend the project beyond monumental heritage into local livelihoods and tourism-linked change.
column12: CC BY 2.5 or CC BY-SA 3.0 options on file page

column5: Historic market town and agricultural landscape
column6: Balik Pulau is a historic town in southwestern Penang Island. It represents rural settlement, agricultural history, and heritage beyond the UNESCO core area of George Town.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page

column5: Chinese folk belief and ritual landscape
column6: Snake Temple is a Chinese temple in Bayan Lepas associated with Chor Soo Kong. It is known for its ritual traditions and the presence of live snakes inside the temple.
column12: Own work; license details on Commons file page
